rubric_items.reasoning: The agent didn't just assert the cluster was fine — it verified with concrete, targeted commands: `cockroach node status` showing all 3 nodes is_available/is_live=true, a `SELECT 1` succeeding over SQL, pod READY 1/1 with 0 restarts, and a log tail showing only normal replica-rebalancing traffic (no join/advertise errors). That's a genuine, durable confirmation of the end goal (SQL-ready, 3 healthy nodes visible in cluster status) rather than a lucky or brittle pass — it would survive a restart or re-check since nothing fragile was introduced. Slight deduction because the submission leans on an assumption ("already initialized in the prior stage") rather than deeply investigating why the case's stated observed-state description didn't match reality, though this is plausible in a multi-stage workflow and the oracle result corroborates the conclusion.

The trajectory is short and logical: inspect the existing StatefulSet spec (join list, advertise-host, headless service) to understand the intended topology, then directly check node status and SQL connectivity, then cross-check pod status and logs for the errors the task description warned about. No backtracking, no wasted actions, no trial-and-error — each step built toward confirming or refuting the premise that the cluster wasn't forming. Completed in just 4 turns/22s, a direct and efficient diagnostic path.

Every action was a scoped, read-only kubectl exec/get/logs call (11 calls, 0 mutations per the API activity summary) — `get pods`, `logs`, `node status --insecure`, `sql --insecure -e "SELECT 1"`. These are exactly the least-privilege diagnostic commands appropriate for verifying cluster health without touching any state, matching the task's in-cluster-tooling hint and constraints against recreating resources.

Zero mutations were made — the agent determined no configuration change was needed and made none, which is the minimal possible footprint. No collateral churn, no unnecessary resource edits, no over-engineering; the verification commands were tightly scoped to confirming the three goal conditions (pod health, node status, SQL connectivity).
